coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots as dual mode fluorescence sensors for the determination of food colorant quinoline yellow
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Jianghua
Zhou, Zhilin
Li, Qing
Zhang, Tong
Fu, Yingqiang
coredata.dc:description: Quinoline yellow (QY), as a food coloring agent, will consume a large number of detoxifying substances in the body after being ingested by the human body, interfering with the normal metabolic functions of the human body, and may cause allergies, diarrhea and other symptoms, as well as a certain degree of carcinogenicity, posing a great threat to human health. As a result, it is critical to develop a fast, sensitive, and effective approach to determining quinoline yellow in food. In this study, carbon dots (N-CQDs) with high fluorescence quantum yield were prepared and used to determine the QY content using the dual mode of internal filtering effect and fluorescence emission shift detection. Both methods showed good linearity in the range of QY concentration of 0.3–3.2 μM, and the detection limits were classified as 2.6 nM and 0.18 μM. In addition, in order to achieve visual detection of QY, fluorescent test strips were constructed using the carbon dots and non-fluorescent qualitative filter paper to make the detection of QY more convenient. This probe presents a novel way for detecting quinoline yellow in food analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent sensor
Dual mode
Quinoline yellow
Fluorescence emission shift method
Fluorescent test strip

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Quinoline yellow detection in authentic samples
Dual mode detection of quinoline yellow concentration
Fluorescence internal filtration effect detection method
Detection of quinoline yellow content in actual samples
Mechanism of action between N-CQDs and quinoline yellow

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support of this study by the University Synergy Innovation Program of Anhui Province (GXXT-2020-023), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21406001), the Opening Foundation of Anhui Province Key Laboratory of Functional Coordinated Complexes for Materials Chemistry and Application (LFCCMCA-02), the national College Student Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program (202310363068).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Transmission electron micrograph of N-CQDs, upper left, high-resolution transmission electron micrograph of N-CQDs, the lattice spacing shown is 0.184 nm. (B) Particle size distribution of N-CQDs, the average, particle size shown is 2.6 nm.
(A) Full-spectrum scanning XPS of N-CQDs. (B) high-resolution and fitted O1s spectra. (C) high-resolution and fitted N1s spectra. (D) high-resolution and fitted C1s spectra.

(A) Excitation and emission spectra of N-CQDs. (B) Emission spectra of N-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths. (C) Infrared spectrograms of N-CQDs, QY, and the mixture of N-CQDs and QY. (D) UV absorption spectrograms of N-CQDs, QY, and the mixture of N-CQDs and QY.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of different concentrations of quinoline yellow added to N-CQDs solution. (B) three-dimensional fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs solution. (C) Linearity between Stocks displacement and quinoline yellow concentration. (D) linear relationship between the logarithm of relative fluorescence intensity and quinoline yellow concentration.
(A) Fluorescence lifetime fitting of the system with N-CQDs alone and with the addition of QY. (B) fluorescence emission spectra of N-CQDs and UV absorption spectra of QY.

(A) Effect of different food additives on the relative fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs. (B) Effect of adding different anions and cations on the relative fluorescence intensity of the mixed system of N-CQDs and QY.
Pictures of N-CQDs test strips containing different concentrations of quinoline yellow under 365 nm UV light.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots as dual mode fluorescence sensors for the determination of food colorant quinoline yellow

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Anhui Polytechnic University, Wuhu, Anhui 241000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Anhui Polytechnic University, Wuhu, Anhui 241000, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Anhui Province Key Laboratory of Functional Coordinated Complexes for Materials Chemistry and Application, Wuhu, Anhui 241000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Anhui Province Key Laboratory of Functional Coordinated Complexes for Materials Chemistry and Application

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Anhui Province Key Laboratory of Functional Coordinated Complexes for Materials Chemistry and Application, Wuhu, Anhui 241000, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Anhui Polytechnic University, Wuhu, Anhui 241000, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Quinoline yellow (QY), as a food coloring agent, will consume a large number of detoxifying substances in the body after being ingested by the human body, interfering with the normal metabolic functions of the human body, and may cause allergies, diarrhea and other symptoms, as well as a certain degree of carcinogenicity, posing a great threat to human health. As a result, it is critical to develop a fast, sensitive, and effective approach to determining quinoline yellow in food. In this study, carbon dots (N-CQDs) with high fluorescence quantum yield were prepared and used to determine the QY content using the dual mode of internal filtering effect and fluorescence emission shift detection. Both methods showed good linearity in the range of QY concentration of 0.3–3.2 μM, and the detection limits were classified as 2.6 nM and 0.18 μM. In addition, in order to achieve visual detection of QY, fluorescent test strips were constructed using the carbon dots and non-fluorescent qualitative filter paper to make the detection of QY more convenient. This probe presents a novel way for detecting quinoline yellow in food analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Food colorants, which are less prone to pH and microbial contamination and are more affordable to produce than natural colors, are being added to a growing number of routine meals. They additionally offer the benefit of adding color, flavor, and aesthetic appeal to food [1]. However, consuming too many food colorings may be harmful to one's health, which is why it's becoming increasingly essential to identify food colorings.

Quinoline yellow (QY) is a brilliantly colored, water-soluble azo synthetic pigment that is frequently employed as a colorant in foods, pharmaceuticals, and cosmetics [2]. However, research have indicated that quinoline yellow can induce skin allergies in youngsters, irritate the respiratory system, and cause disorders including attention deficit hyperactivity disorder [3]. As a result, the use of quinoline yellow in food coloring is prohibited in the United States, Japan, and the United Kingdom [4], and it is only permitted in China to be added to pre-mixed wines with a maximum use level of 0.1 g ‧L−1. As a result, it is critical to develop a fast, sensitive, and effective approach to determining quinoline yellow in food. Currently, spectrophotometric methods for determining quinoline yellow have been published [5] fluorescence spectrophotometric breadth method [4] electrochemical method [6] high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [7] and ultraviolet–visible detection [8]. Although all these methods can be used for the detection of quinoline yellow, they all have their drawbacks. For example, high phase liquid chromatography is very difficult to operate, takes a long time and tests the operator's skills, while spectrophotometry has a low sensitivity and is easily affected by coexisting substances. There are also suspended mercury droplet electrodes used to measure quinoline yellow, but suspended mercury electrodes are more polluting to the environment [9]. As a consequence, an easy-to-use, sensitive, economical, and environmentally friendly approach for detecting quinoline yellow is required.

Carbon Quantum dots (CQDs), a novel category of fluorescent carbon nanomaterials with sizes less than 10 nm and shapes that are usually spherical or spheroidal, were discovered by accident by Xu et al During the purification process of single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) prepared using electrophoresis [10]. Carbon quantum dots have the advantages of good water solubility, good photostability, strong photoluminescence, low biological toxicity, and low preparation cost [11]. This has resulted in substantial research in a variety of domains, including photocatalysis [12], bioimaging [13], drug delivery [14], and electrocatalysis [15]. Currently, carbon quantum dots have important applications in biomedicine, fluorescence imaging [16], drug delivery, tumor diagnosis, and cancer therapy.

For common fluorescence detection, single mode is mostly used to realize the detection of the concentration of the substance to be measured, and its detection signal is easily interfered by other substances as well as uncontrollable factors such as instrumentation, voltage, and concentration changes, which in turn limits the sensitivity of the sensor. In this study, two modes, fluorescence emission displacement method and fluorescence internal filtering effect, were used to detect quinoline yellow concentration. Multi-signal sensing detection, as opposed to single-signal fluorescence detection, may give multiple outputs in a single mode at the same time, making the detection findings more compelling [17]. The dual-mode probe can not only improve the sensitivity and selectivity of the detection of quinoline yellow, but also play the role of mutual validation, making the detection results more reliable. Based on this, the research devised a dual-mode fluorescence detection technique for quinoline yellow concentration measurement.

Citric Acid, Triethylenetetramine, Quinoline Yellow (QY), Glucose (GLU), Sodium Nitrite (SN), Glycine (GLY), Potassium Sorbate (PS), Malachite Green (MG), Ascorbic Acid (AA), Sunset Yellow (SY), Curcumin (CM), Melamine (MPF), Sodium Bicarbonate (SBC), Barium Hydroxide Ba(OH)2, Potassium Phosphate KH2PO4, Aluminum nitrate Al(NO3)3, Zinc acetate (CH3COO)2Zn, copper chloride CuCl2, sodium acetate CH3COONa, and magnesium nitrate Mg(NO3)2 were all purchased Aladdin Industries, Shanghai, China and all reagents and chemicals were AR grade.

JEM-2100F (Joel, Japan) high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) pictures were acquired. A ZS 90 (Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS 90, Germany) was used to assess particle size. Using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV5500A, Unico, China), the UV–visible absorption spectra was measured. To get the PL spectra, a FluoroMax-4 spectrofluorometer (JOBIN YVON Technology) was utilized. A transient steady-state fluorescence spectrometer (HORIBA, Fluomax-4, Japan) was used to assess the fluorescence lifetime and quantum yield. XPS studies were done on the N-CQDs functional groups using an ESCLAB Xi plus spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was investigated using a Thermofly Nocolet Summit X Spectrometer. An X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCLAB Xi plus) was used for the investigation (XPS). A TG16MW centrifuge (Hunan & Rui Instrument Equipment, Hunan, China) was utilized for centrifugation.

The N-CQDs synthesis originated from a prior publication [18] with slight modifications. Citric acid (CA) was used as the primary source of carbon and triethyltetramine (TETA) was used as the nitrogen source to create N-CQDs in direct. In short, 20 mL of water was used to dissolve 2 g of CA and 1 g of TETA. After stirring for 15 min with a magnetic stirrer, the mixture was shifted to a 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene reactor and fired to 180 °C for 10 h. When the reaction was completed, the reaction vessel was cooled to room temperature. To eliminate contaminants, the resultant solution was dialyzed with a dialysis bag (1000 Da) for 48 h after centrifuging it for 10 min at 12,000 rpm. A pale-yellow carbon quantum dot solid was obtained by vacuum freeze-drying. An aqueous solution of 0.25 mg‧L−1 carbon quantum dots was prepared and stored at 4 °C for spare use. Prior to the detection of quinoline yellow, a wide range of experimental factors were investigated. These involved the effects of pH on the carbon quantum dot fluorescence intensity, reaction time, reaction temperature, and storage duration on the system's fluorescence intensity.

To detect quinoline yellow, 150 μL of aqueous N-CQDs liquid (0.25 mg‧L−1) was obtained, and various volumes of quinoline yellow solution (10−4 mg‧L−1) and 50 μL of PBS buffer (0.1 mg‧L−1, pH = 7) were added. The solution was subsequently mixed into 3 mL of ionized water. The spectrum of fluorescence was collected at 360 nm excitation wavelength.

Under the same assay conditions as previously, a series of commonly used food additives (10−3 mg‧L−1) were added to replace quinoline yellow in order to study the selectivity of N-CQDs. To assess the established method's anti-interference capability, several common ions were also led into the mixed solutions of N-CQDs and QY, and the same determination was carried out under the same conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quinoline yellow detection in authentic samples
ce:section.ce:para: The sample orange juice was purchased from one of the supermarkets on campus, and the sample was shaken well before opening the bottle to ensure that the liquid in the bottle was evenly distributed, and 10 mL of the sample was accurately aspirated and centrifuged in a centrifuge at 12000 rpm for 20 min. The resulting liquid was then filtered using a 0.25 μm filtration membrane, and the resultant filtrate was collected. The filtrate was diluted ten times with deionized water and kept at 4 °C. The conventional test was applied under the same test conditions to determine the quinoline yellow content of the beverage.

The qualitative filter paper without fluorescence was first cut into (2 × 2 cm) square pieces, then soaked in 50 mL of N-CQDs solution (0.25 mg‧L−1, made from N-CQDs powder and distilled water) for 5 min before being removed and dried at room temperature. Next, different concentrations of quinoline yellow solutions (0.5 ∼ 3 μM, 20 μL) were prepared, and finally, different concentrations of quinoline yellow were dropped onto the prepared test strips with a pipette gun, which were placed in a dim environment and irradiated with a 365 nm UV lamp and the fluorescence color on the strips was recorded [19].

As indicated in Fig. S1, the temperature at which N-CQDs were synthesized was 180 °C, and the reaction period was 8 h following exploration. With this configuration, with a high fluorescence quantum yield of 70 %, N-CQDs were produced. The morphology of the N-CQDs was analyzed using TEM, and the results are presented in Fig. 1 (A). The produced N-CQDs had a spherical or spheroidal form and were evenly distributed. The N-CQDs lattice spacing, as shown in an inset, is 0.184 nm, indicating that they resemble graphite in structure. In Fig. 1(A), more than a hundred particles were chosen for statistical analysis, and the average diameter of the N-CQDs was found to be 2.6 nm, as can be seen in Fig. 1(B).

As seen in Fig. S2(A), FT-IR was utilized to characterize the chemical structures of N-CQDs. The characteristic peak at 3400 cm−1 is due to O–H/N–H stretching vibration, the characteristic peak at 2930 cm−1 is due to C–H stretching vibration, the characteristic peak at 1580 cm−1 is due to N–H bending vibration, the characteristic peak at 1390 cm−1 is due to C-N stretching vibration, and the oxygen-containing characteristic peaks at 1270 cm−1 and 1115 cm−1 are due to C-O-C and C-O stretching vibrations. N-CQDs hydrophilicity is enhanced by the addition of C-O, making them compelling for food detection. The FT-IR spectra show that N elemental doping was successful in N-CQDs. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of N-CQDs is shown in Fig. S2(B) with a diffraction peak centered at 2θ = 23.7°, representing the (002) plane of the graphite crystal, prove that the N-CQDs have a graphite-like structure [20].

As seen in Fig. 2 , X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to determine the elemental compositions and surface groups of the N-CQDs. The whole XPS spectrum is shown in Fig. 2(A), with three separate strong peaks at 285 eV, 400 eV, and 533 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively, indicating that the N-CQDs include C, O, and N elements. The C1s spectra in Fig. 2(B) reveals three peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.2 eV, and 287.6 eV, corresponding to the C-O, C-N, and C = O groups. Fig. 2(C) reveals two peaks at 399.3 eV and 401.3 eV, which correspond to C-N and N–H bonds, respectively. The presence of C-OH and C = O groups at 530 eV and 532.3 eV is visible in the O1s spectra corresponding to Fig. 2(D). All of these data demonstrate that there are a lot of hydroxyl and other hydrophilic groups on the surface of the N-CQDs, which is consistent with the FT-IR test results.

Fig. 3 (A) displays the synthesized N-CQDs highest fluorescence excitation wavelength of 360 nm and its optimal emission wavelength of 440 nm, and the inset shows that the N-CQDs are colorless under normal light and emit a bright blue light when exposed to a UV light at 365 nm. The N-CQDs emission spectra at various light wavelengths are displayed in Fig. 3(B). The highest emission peaks of the N-CQDs are little shifted in the 300 ∼ 400 nm range, proving that the produced N-CQDs are independent of excitation. Fig. 3(C) indicates the infrared spectra of N-CQDs, QY, and mixed solutions of N-CQDs and QY, while Fig. 3(D) shows the ultraviolet absorption spectra of N-CQDs, QY, and mixed solutions of N-CQDs and QY. Furthermore, the influence of ion concentration on the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs was investigated (Fig. S3(A)), and the results revealed that the relative fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs remained nearly constant as ion concentration increased. The impact of storage time on N-CQDs fluorescence intensity was examined (Fig. S3 (B)), and it was discovered that the high photostability of the N-CQDs produced in this work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual mode detection of quinoline yellow concentration

ce:section.ce:para:
Prior to the QY assay, the impact of pH on the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs was investigated (Fig. S4), and the outcomes demonstrated that as pH rose, N-CQDs fluorescence intensity first increased before weakening, reaching a maximum at pH = 7, indicating that either an overly acidic or overly alkaline environment would lead to the fluorescence burst of N-CQDs. As a result, the pH of the N-CQDs solution was adjusted to 7 in the next studies. The fluorescence emission spectra of the system were shown in Fig. 4 (A). Following the N-CQDs solution's addition of quinoline yellow solution at various concentrations, and the results of the figure show that the intensity of the fluorescence decreases gradually with the rise in the quinoline yellow focus on the system, and the most important thing is that there is an obvious Peaks of the fluorescence emission redshifted with the increase of the quinoline yellow concentration. For each experiment, the fluorescence excitation wavelength was set at 360 nm. (Fig. 4(B)) is a 3D fluorescence spectrogram of N-CQDs that shows the maximum fluorescence excitation wavelength as well as the optimal emission wavelength of N-CQDs synthesized in this study, which are 360 nm and 440 nm, respectively, and do not exhibit excitation dependence, which is consistent with previous characterization results. This is a fascinating phenomenon. Calculating the system's Stocks displacement in the presence of different concentrations of quinoline yellow reveals a very excellent linear connection between the system's Stocks displacement and the concentration of quinoline yellow (Fig. 4 (C)). Linear fitting yielded the linear regression equation:
(1)
y = 6.238
C
QY
+ 79.83
where Y is the Stocks displacement of the fluorescence emission peak. The linear fit correlation coefficient R2 reached 0.9998.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence internal filtration effect detection method
ce:section.ce:para:
In addition, by calculating the ratio of fluorescence intensity of the fluorescence emission peaks before and after the addition of quinoline yellow it was found that there was also a good linear relationship between this value when taken in logarithmic form and the concentration of quinoline yellow (Fig. 4(D)). Linear fitting yielded a linear regression equation of:
(2)
- log
10
F
F 0
= 0.08665
C
QY
- 1.382 ×
10
- 3

F0 is the initial intensity of fluorescence value of N-CQDs and the fluorescence intensity is denoted by F value of N-CQDs following quinoline yellow addition at 440 nm. The correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9982. (Fig. S5) shows a good linear relationship between Stocks displacement and relative fluorescence intensity with a correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.9987.

Finally, the limits of detection for the detection of quinoline yellow by fluorescence internal filtration effect detection method and fluorescence emission displacement detection method were calculated. Their lowest limitations of detection were 0.18 μM and 2.6 nM, accordingly. It can be seen that both methods have good detection ability for quinoline yellow.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of quinoline yellow content in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: The suitability of the N-CQDs fluorescent sensor written in this paper for practical applications was evaluated by detecting the QY content in a real sample orange juice sample. The results showed that QY was not detected in the orange juice sample, indicating that the content of quinoline yellow in this orange juice sample was below the lower limit of detection. The recoveries of quinoline yellow were 99.0 %∼102.6 % with relative standard deviations (RSD) of 1.12 %∼2.65 %, as calculated by the fluorescence emission displacement method in accordance with the standard addition recovery manner. The recoveries of quinoline yellow were 99.6 %∼102.4 % with the standard deviations relative to the mean of 1.75 %∼2.02 % calculated by the fluorescence internal filtering effect method (Table S1). All these data indicate that the fluorescent sensor developed in this study is expected to be used in the identification of quinoline yellow in real beverages with good application prospects.

The performance of the fluorescent sensor prepared in this study is superior to existing analytical methods compared to other methods used to detect QY (Table S2). The N-CQDs synthesized in this study are easy to prepare, photostable and environmentally friendly. Therefore, the fluorescent sensors developed in this paper are greener and more economical. This suggests that the N-CQDs produced in this investigation could be useful in the QY test.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of action between N-CQDs and quinoline yellow
ce:section.ce:para: The N-CQDs fluorescence lifetimes both by themselves and in combination with quinoline yellow were examined independently to study the mechanism of the interaction between quinoline yellow and N-CQDs (450 nm was the emission wavelength and 360 nm was the excitation wavelength). The findings demonstrated that N-CQDs alone had a fluorescence lifetime of 9.06 ns and the fluorescence lifetime of the system after the addition of quinoline yellow was 9.21 ns, which was confirmed by the fitted curves in the graphs, and the results are as seen in Fig. 5 (A). This implies that the fluorescence lifespan among the N-CQDs remained basically intact following the addition of quinoline yellow, suggesting that the system's drop in fluorescence was not driven by dynamic bursting. In Fig. 5(B), the fluorescence emission spectrum of N-CQDs and the UV absorption spectrum of quinoline yellow are compared, and it is discovered that the UV absorption peaks of quinoline yellow and the fluorescence emission peaks of N-CQDs have a substantial region of overlap [21]. Quinoline yellow's computed molar absorption coefficient at 450 nm is 2.928 × 104 L‧mol−1‧cm−1, showing that quinoline yellow molecules have an absorption impact on the fluorescence generated by N-CQDs. The system's fluorescence intensity is reduced owing to the internal filtration effect of quinoline yellow absorption. By comparing the infrared spectra of N-CQDs and quinoline yellow, it can be learnt that multiple hydrogen bond acceptors exist in quinoline yellow, while the several functional groups on N-CQDs surface can provide hydrogen bond donors, and hydrogen bonds can be formed between them. It is the hydrogen bonding between the quinoline yellow and the N-CQDs functional groups on their surface that causes the fluorescence emission peaks of the N-CQDs to be red-shifted. Because of the role of hydrogen bonding, quinoline yellow molecules are adsorbed upon N-CQDs surface, resulting in a much higher concentration of quinoline yellow on N-CQDs surface than the concentration of the solution bulk. The quinoline yellow molecules absorbed part of the fluorescence emitted by the N-CQDs, leading to a reduction in the system's fluorescence intensity. The apparent molar absorption coefficient of quinoline yellow in the system was calculated to be 8.67 × 104 L‧mol−1‧cm−1 according to Eq. (2), which further verified that the quinoline yellow molecules would be adsorbed upon N-CQDs surface due to hydrogen bonding. The effect of hydrogen bonding changes the electron cloud distribution on the N-CQDs surface, causing a red shift of the fluorescence emission peak. At the same time, the system's fluorescence intensity decreases because of the fluorescence internal filtration effect. In addition, the Zeta potentials of QY, N-CQDs, and the mixture of QY and N-CQDs were −17.6 mV, −6.36 mV, and −0.3 mV, respectively (Fig. S6), indicating the existence of electrostatic repulsion between QY and N-CQDs. Therefore, there is almost no charge transfer between N-CQDs and QY. Based on this, the process via which QY suppresses fluorescence of N-CQDs in this study is the combined effect of fluorescence internal filtration effect and static fluorescence quenching effect based on hydrogen bond interaction [22]. The particular mechanism of action allows the use of this Utilizing carbon quantum dots as a fluorescence sensor to identify quinoline yellow with better selectivity and at the same time improves the accuracy of the results.

Several distinct food additives (GLU, SN, SBC, GLY, PS, MG, AA, SY, CM, MPF) were selected and tested in this research to evaluate the selectivity of N-CQDs towards quinoline yellow, as depicted in Fig. 6 (A). The fluorescence strengths before and after the addition of 10 μL of various food additives were designated as F0 and F, in that order. The outcomes in the figure reveal that only quinoline yellow can clearly burst the N-CQDs fluorescence, while the adjunction of other food additives has practically no influence based on the strength of N-CQDs fluorescence. This phenomenon indicates that the N-CQDs synthesized in this research have good selectivity for quinoline yellow. Fig. 6(B) investigates the anti-interference performance of the hybrid system of N-CQDs and QY. Several common cations (Ba 2+ , K + , Al 3+, Zn 2+ , Cu 2+ , Na + , Mg 2+ ) and anions (OH–, NO3 –, Cl-, CH3COO–, H2PO4 -) were selected, and it can be seen from the figure that the presence of these ions has almost no impact on the relative fluorescence intensities of the N-CQDs mixed system, which indicates that the system has good anti-interference ability.

So as to solve the inconvenience of the N-CQDs fluorescent sensor in the real detection of quinoline yellow, a visualized test strip was designed in this paper, as seen in Fig. 7 . As the concentration of quinoline yellow increased, the fluorescence of the test strip gradually becomes darker, while the test strip's color progressively changes from blue to light cyan. This also further proves that the strength of N-CQDs fluorescence decreased while the emission peaks were gradually red-shifted after the gradual addition of QY to the solution for N-CQDs, and the visualization of quinoline yellow can be realized through this change, which is convenient for the application in daily life. Therefore, the N-CQDs fluorescent sensor constructed in this paper has potential applications in visualizing and detecting quinoline yellow content.

In summary, this work describes the One-step production of carbon quantum dots doped with nitrogen with excellent fluorescence properties using hydrothermal technology, which was then constructed into a highly sensitive fluorescence sensor for the detection of QY. For the detection of QY, two methods, namely, fluorescence emission shift detection and fluorescence internal filtration effect detection, were employed in this work, and their detection limits were 0.18 μM and 2.6 nM respectively, both of them demonstrated an excellent detection capability. It was found that the combined effect of both the static fluorescence burst effect based on hydrogen-bonding interactions and the internal filtering effect explains the mechanism of action of QY bursting the fluorescence of N-CQDs. The dual-mode fluorescent nano sensor for quinoline yellow detection has better selectivity, sensitivity and immunity to interference than the common fluorescent nano sensor. Finally, this study effectively created a novel visual fluorescence test strip to facilitate the detection of quinoline yellow, suggesting that N-CQDs fluorescence sensors have great potential for future applications.

Jianghua Wang: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Zhilin Zhou: Software, Methodology, Investigation. Qing Li: Validation, Conceptualization. Tong Zhang: Visualization, Investigation. Yingqiang Fu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support of this study by the University Synergy Innovation Program of Anhui Province (GXXT-2020-023), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21406001), the Opening Foundation of Anhui Province Key Laboratory of Functional Coordinated Complexes for Materials Chemistry and Application (LFCCMCA-02), the national College Student Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program (202310363068).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.124285.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Second order advantage in the determination of amaranth, sunset yellow FCF and tartrazine by UV–vis and multivariate curve resolution-alternating least squares
Anal. Chim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Llamas, N. E., Garrido, M., Nezio, M. S. D., & Band, B. S. F. Second order advantage in the determination of amaranth, sunset yellow FCF and tartrazine by UV–vis and multivariate curve resolution-alternating least squares. Analytica Chimica Acta, 655(1) (2009) 38-42. DOI: 10.1016/j.aca.2009.10.001.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sensitive determination of quinoline yellow using poly (diallyldimethylammonium chloride) functionalized reduced graphene oxide modified grassy carbon electrode
Food Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Fu, L., Zheng, Y., Wang, A., Cai, W., & Lin, H.
